answer:
\boxed{C}

The birth of **modern English** is traditionally associated with the **Norman Conquest of 1066 c.e.**, when French and Latin influences reshaped the language, marking a shift from Old English to Middle English. While Old English (pre-1066) and Middle English (post-1066) are distinct, the foundational linguistic changes that laid the groundwork for modern English began around this time. Later dates (e.g., 1492 or 1588) mark other significant events (Columbus, Armada) but are not the primary linguistic turning points.
